class_schedule.topic: PM Tool Landscape and Selection Criteria
learning_objectives: Categorize project management tools by function and scale
Define selection criteria for PM software
Evaluate tools against organizational needs using a decision matrix
readings: Gartner, Magic Quadrant for Project and Portfolio Management (latest)
PMI Pulse of the Profession: Technology Trends

lecture_notes.introduction: The right tool amplifies the PM's effectiveness. The wrong tool creates overhead without value. Selection must match organizational needs.
key_concepts: Tool categories: scheduling, collaboration, portfolio management, resource management, agile/Kanban
Enterprise vs. team tools: Microsoft Project/Primavera vs. Jira/Asana/Monday.com
Selection criteria: features, usability, integration, scalability, cost, support, adoption effort
Decision matrix approach: weighted scoring of criteria against candidate tools
Total cost of ownership: license, implementation, training, customization, ongoing support
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating three PM tools (Microsoft Project, Jira, and Monday.com) using a weighted decision matrix for a mid-size IT company
setup_requirements: Free trial accounts for 3 PM tools

review.value: Start with needs, not features; the best tool is the one your team will actually use
License cost is often less than 30% of total cost; implementation, training, and customization dominate

class_schedule.topic: Microsoft Project: Advanced Scheduling and Tracking
learning_objectives: Build complex schedules with multiple calendars and constraints
Set baselines and track progress using percent complete and actual dates
Generate variance reports and earned value data from Microsoft Project
readings: Microsoft Project Step by Step, Ch. 6-10
Stover, Microsoft Project Tips and Tricks

lecture_notes.introduction: Microsoft Project remains the standard for detailed schedule management. Mastering its advanced features separates competent schedulers from novices.
key_concepts: Task types: fixed duration, fixed work, fixed units and their impact on scheduling
Multiple baselines: baselining the original plan and re-baselining after approved changes
Progress tracking: percent complete, actual start/finish, remaining duration
Custom fields and formulas: creating calculated fields for project-specific metrics
Earned value fields: BCWS, BCWP, ACWP, CV, SV available natively
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tracking a project through 3 status periods in Microsoft Project, updating actuals and analyzing variances

review.value: Fixed Duration keeps the timeline; Fixed Work keeps the effort; Fixed Units keeps the resource allocation; changing one calculates the others
The tracking Gantt overlay shows baseline bars vs. actual bars; visual identification of schedule slippage

class_schedule.topic: Agile Tools: Jira and Azure DevOps
learning_objectives: Configure Jira for Scrum and Kanban projects
Set up Azure DevOps for agile work tracking and CI/CD pipeline management
Create dashboards and reports for agile metrics
readings: Atlassian Jira Documentation: Scrum and Kanban Guides
Microsoft Azure DevOps Documentation: Boards Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Agile tools track work at the story level, provide real-time visibility into sprint progress, and integrate with development toolchains.
key_concepts: Jira configuration: project types, issue types (epic/story/task/bug), workflows, boards
Sprint management in Jira: backlog grooming, sprint planning, sprint board, sprint reports
Azure DevOps Boards: work items, iteration paths, area paths, queries
Agile dashboards: velocity charts, cumulative flow diagrams, sprint burndown, cycle time
Integration: connecting agile tools with Git repositories, CI/CD pipelines, and communication tools
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a Scrum project in Jira with a product backlog, sprint board, and velocity tracking

review.value: Custom workflows define the states and transitions work items go through; should match your team's actual process
Shows work distribution across states over time; widening bands indicate bottlenecks

learning_objectives: Configure collaboration platforms for project teams
Integrate project management, communication, and document tools
Design information architecture for project knowledge management

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern project management requires a connected ecosystem of tools, not isolated applications. Integration eliminates manual status updates.
key_concepts: Collaboration platforms: Confluence, SharePoint, Notion for project documentation and knowledge
Communication integration: Slack/Teams channels linked to project milestones and alerts
Automation: Jira-Slack notifications, automated status reports, workflow triggers
Information architecture: structuring project documentation for findability and reuse
Single source of truth: eliminating conflicting versions through linked, live data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an integrated project workspace connecting Jira, Confluence, and Slack with automated notifications

review.value: Project schedule in the tool, not in email; status in the dashboard, not in separate reports; reduces version conflicts
Automate routine notifications and status aggregation; save PM time for decision-making and stakeholder engagement

class_schedule.topic: Portfolio Tools, Reporting, and Data-Driven PM
learning_objectives: Configure portfolio management views in enterprise PM tools
Build executive dashboards from project data
Apply data analytics to project management decision-making
readings: Power BI for Project Management (tutorial guides)

lecture_notes.introduction: Executive stakeholders need aggregated portfolio views, not individual project details. The right reporting tool surfaces the right information at the right level.
key_concepts: Portfolio dashboards: project health summary, resource utilization, budget status, strategic alignment
Executive reporting: RAG status, milestone tracking, risk heatmaps, financial summaries
Power BI / Tableau: connecting to project data sources and building interactive visualizations
Predictive analytics: using historical project data to forecast outcomes
Data quality: garbage in, garbage out; ensuring project data is accurate and current
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a Power BI portfolio dashboard connected to Microsoft Project data with drill-down capability

review.value: Show health status and exceptions, not details; executives need to know which projects need attention, not task-level status
Use historical velocity, estimation accuracy, and risk data to improve future project planning rather than relying on intuition

class_schedule.topic: AI in Project Management and Emerging Tools
learning_objectives: Evaluate AI-powered PM features in modern tools
Understand how AI assists in estimation, risk identification, and resource optimization
Assess the future trajectory of PM technology
readings: PMI AI and Project Management Report (latest)
Gartner Hype Cycle for Project Management Technology

lecture_notes.introduction: AI is transforming project management tools from passive record-keeping to active decision support systems.
key_concepts: AI-assisted estimation: learning from historical data to improve accuracy
Automated risk identification: NLP analysis of project documents and communications
Resource optimization: AI-driven matching of skills, availability, and project needs
Predictive project analytics: forecasting schedule and cost outcomes from current data
Ethical considerations: bias in AI recommendations, data privacy, over-reliance on automation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing AI features across three modern PM platforms and evaluating their practical value
setup_requirements: Access to PM tools with AI features

review.value: AI can identify patterns in historical data that humans miss; useful for calibrating estimates, not replacing expert judgment
AI handles data processing and pattern recognition; humans handle judgment, relationships, and context that AI cannot

questions.question: An organization runs both waterfall construction projects and agile software projects. They want a single PM tool. Explain why this is a poor requirement and recommend a better approach.
questions.answer: A single tool is a poor requirement because waterfall and agile projects have fundamentally different management approaches. Waterfall projects need detailed Gantt charts, CPM scheduling, resource leveling, and earned value tracking (strengths of Microsoft Project/Primavera). Agile projects need sprint boards, backlog management, velocity tracking, and burndown charts (strengths of Jira/Azure DevOps). No single tool excels at both. Better approach: use the right tool for each methodology (e.g., Microsoft Project for construction, Jira for software) but invest in a portfolio-level tool or dashboard (e.g., Power BI or a PPM tool) that aggregates data from both for executive reporting and resource management.

rubric.key: Explains why single tool fails for both approaches
Recommends a practical multi-tool approach with integration

questions.question: In Microsoft Project, explain the difference between Fixed Duration, Fixed Work, and Fixed Units task types, and describe a scenario where each is appropriate.
questions.answer: Fixed Duration: the task takes a set amount of time regardless of resources assigned. Adding resources reduces work per person but not the elapsed time. Scenario: a concrete cure time of 7 days; adding more workers does not make concrete cure faster. Fixed Work: the total effort is constant. Adding resources reduces the duration. Scenario: a data entry task requiring 80 hours of work; assigning 2 people at 100% completes it in 40 hours instead of 80. Fixed Units: the resource assignment percentage is constant. Changing duration changes work, or vice versa. Scenario: a consultant is available 50% of their time; changing the task duration automatically adjusts the total hours at the fixed 50% rate.

rubric.key: Correctly defines all three task types

questions.question: A project team using Jira has a velocity chart showing: Sprint 1: 20 points, Sprint 2: 35 points, Sprint 3: 18 points, Sprint 4: 33 points. What does this data tell you about the team, and what would you investigate?
questions.answer: The velocity shows high variability (18-35, nearly a 2x range) rather than stabilization. This suggests: (1) Story point estimation may be inconsistent (stories are not sized relative to each other), (2) The team may be committing to different amounts of work each sprint, (3) External disruptions may be pulling team members away intermittently, (4) Scope changes mid-sprint could be inflating or deflating completed points. Investigation: (1) Review story point estimation practices, (2) Check if sprint scope changed after planning, (3) Assess if the same team members were available each sprint, (4) Look at the Definition of Done consistency. The team should not use this velocity for reliable forecasting until it stabilizes.

rubric.key: Identifies high variability as the key issue

questions.question: Design a project dashboard for an executive audience. List 5 metrics you would include and explain why each matters at the executive level.
questions.answer: 1) Overall project RAG status (Red/Amber/Green): instant visual health assessment, executives see immediately which projects need attention. 2) Schedule variance (weeks ahead/behind): quantifies schedule health in terms executives understand; directly impacts business commitments. 3) Budget consumed vs. budget planned (%): shows financial health; triggers early intervention if spending exceeds plan. 4) Top 3 risks with impact and mitigation status: executives need to know what could go wrong and what is being done about it. 5) Key milestone timeline (planned vs. actual): shows whether major deliverables are on track; milestones map to business decisions and dependencies.

rubric.key: Explains why each matters to executives specifically
Metrics are actionable, not just informational

major_assignments.title: PM Tool Implementation and Dashboard Project
major_assignments.description: Select, configure, and populate a PM tool for a realistic project scenario. Create an integrated workspace with scheduling, collaboration, and executive reporting capabilities.
objectives: Select and justify a PM tool for a given scenario
Configure the tool with a realistic project including schedule, resources, and backlog
Build an executive dashboard with key metrics
Demonstrate tool integration with communication and documentation platforms
deliverables: Tool selection analysis with decision matrix (2-3 pages)
Configured PM tool with 30+ tasks/stories, resources, and dependencies (screenshots + export)
Executive dashboard with 5+ metrics and drill-down capability (live or screenshot + explanation)
Integration demonstration showing PM tool connected to at least 2 other platforms (video or screenshots)
Tool configuration guide for future administrators (2-3 pages)

criteria: Decision matrix uses relevant, weighted criteria
Project is realistically populated with tasks, resources, and dependencies
Tool features are used effectively (not just basic task listing)
Workflows, custom fields, or views are configured for the team's needs
Visual design is clear and professional
Data is live or realistically simulated
At least 2 integrations demonstrated and working
Configuration guide is clear enough for another administrator to follow
Integration adds real value to the workflow

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on tool selection, configuration, and reporting

tool_project.description: PM tool implementation and dashboard project

midterm_examination.description: Tool selection criteria and Microsoft Project proficiency

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all PM tools and reporting topics

lab_exercises.description: Hands-on lab exercises with each tool platform

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. Missing a lab without prior approval results in zero credit for that lab.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. Tool configurations must be accessible for grading at the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be done in pairs. The tool implementation project is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All tool configurations must be your own work. Using pre-configured templates without modification is not acceptable.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to PM tool free tiers (Jira, Azure DevOps, Monday.com). Microsoft Project desktop or Online. Power BI Desktop (free). Reliable internet for cloud-based tools.
